nodes.text: XIX — The Sun

A note on the chronology: this chapter, like Temperance, takes place several years after the events of the chapters that precede it. The Sun is the card that comes after the Moon not in time but in sequence. Sequence, as the deck has been reminding us, is not chronology. Sequence is the order in which the cards agree to be read.

It was a Tuesday morning in late May, and John Frank was at Café Olimpico on Saint-Viateur.

He had been coming here on Tuesdays for six years. The café had been here for fifty. The staff, by now, did not ask him what he wanted; they made him the single espresso he had started ordering in the first week, and set it down beside the small water glass he had not asked for but had been given, and went back to their own work. The transaction was the same transaction it had been for six years. The transaction was, by this point, more than a transaction. It was something closer to climate.

He sat at the small round metal table in the back corner, under the window, with his notebook open. A shaft of late-morning sunlight fell across the table, through the window, in the specific diagonal slant Montreal did in late May, and the dust motes — Café Olimpico had dust motes, and had had them for fifty years, and the dust motes were part of what you paid for — performed their intricate silent ballet in the shaft.

He watched the dust motes for a long time.

He had been watching them, in one form or another, for six years. He had been thinking, for most of those years, that they were random. He had been thinking, in the past three weeks, that they were not.

Elicia was, at this moment, twelve minutes late.

Which was — to be fair, if fairness still applied to Elicia Duchane and her relationship with the concept of time — not late by any standard Elicia recognized as binding. Elicia had, in her Graustark years, once arrived to a scheduled interview at her own apartment forty minutes after the interviewer had given up and left. Elicia had, in her Montreal years, begun to improve on this, but the improvement had been incremental and punctuated by reversals, and John had adopted, after the second time she had stood him up, the quiet protective stance of arriving on time himself and then using the waiting period for his own work, so that if Elicia did not arrive he had at least not lost the hour.

He had, on average, used this technique for the past three months. Elicia had, on average, arrived. He had stopped, sometime around month two, pretending he would not wait.

She had answered his three-week-old email, seven months ago, with one word: Yes.

He had, the following Tuesday, arrived at the café at ten. She had arrived at ten forty-three. She had looked, when she came in through the door, like a woman who had, in the recent past, been inside a building that was collapsing and who had, on exiting the building, not yet fully understood that she was still covered in dust.

She had sat across from him. She had ordered an espresso. She had said, without preamble:

I am broke, I am evicted in twenty-three days, I have been filming myself every night for the past eleven days, I think I may finally be making a thing that is mine, and I have not had a real conversation with anyone in fourteen months. I am here because you wrote me a kind email and I don't have the energy to be clever about it. Please do not be kind to me right now. Kind will make me cry. I want to be, instead, dry.

John, who had been preparing, for three weeks, a careful measured opening sentence intended to give Elicia exactly the amount of room she needed, had laughed — a short surprised laugh, the laugh of a man whose carefully prepared strategy had just been rendered unnecessary by the other party's willingness to go first.
Okay. Dry. Noted. How's the coffee.
The coffee is excellent. The chair is terrible. You come here every Tuesday.
I do.
Why.
Because the café has dust motes, and the dust motes have a pattern, and I have, for reasons I decline to fully articulate, been tracking the pattern.
What's the pattern.
It clusters on Tuesdays.
Of course it does.
That had been the first Tuesday. They had been meeting, more or less, every Tuesday since.

The seven months had not fixed Elicia. Seven months did not, John had learned by his late thirties, fix anyone. Seven months could, however, temper — the word that his own card had given him as a gift, the word he had been using for some time as his practical ethic.

Elicia had, in the seven months, been evicted (as predicted). She had crashed at his apartment for two weeks during the transition (against his better judgment; with his full consent; he had taken the futon and given her the bedroom and they had fought, twice, about dishes and both times had apologized the next morning in the careful way adults apologized when they recognized the fight was about something else). She had found a much smaller apartment in Mile End, above a bagel shop, for roughly half what the previous apartment had cost. She had, in the new apartment, continued to film herself.

The footage had accumulated. The footage was, at last count, approximately thirty hours. The footage was, Elicia had confided to him over espresso in March, the most honest thing she had ever made.

She had not, yet, shown any of it to anyone. Not to him. Not to the Roberts, whom she had begun, tentatively, to email. Not to the painter boyfriend (new, cautious, a carpenter named Luc, who worked with his hands and who did not, John understood, know about the footage). Not to anyone.

The footage was waiting. She was waiting with it. The waiting, John had come to recognize, was the project.

He had not, in the seven months, told Elicia about the text from the Texas number.

He had not told her about the reply he had sent (yes.). He had not told her that the thread had gone quiet and then, twice more, pulsed — not with a message, but with the small vibration of the other party having opened the thread, read it, and chosen not to add to it.

He had told her, in a roundabout way, that he had begun, again, to note things. He had said:
The discipline of being temperate turns out to be more interesting when the temperature in the room is changing.
John, my darling, if you have something to say, say it.
I'll say it eventually. I'm not ready.
Elicia had looked at him across the small metal table with the specific attention she used for a shot she was framing in her head.
Fine. But I want to know, when you're ready, that I am the first person you tell.
You will be.
He had meant it.

At ten twenty-three, the bell over the café door chimed.

John looked up. It was not Elicia. It was a young woman he did not recognize. She was tall, blue-dreadlocked hair tucked under a bright yellow rain slicker (though it was not, today, raining), and she was carrying a cardboard tube of the kind artists used to carry rolled canvases. She glanced around the café, spotted what she was looking for — a small table by the front window where a man John had also never seen before was apparently waiting for her — and crossed to him without noticing John at all.

John's eye followed her. He was not quite sure why.

She sat with the man. They began a quiet conversation. The man pulled the cardboard tube toward himself and did not, at any point, open it. They both drank coffee. They both looked, to John's practiced eye, like they were having a meeting that was not quite a date and not quite a transaction, the kind of meeting that falls in the increasingly common genre between the two.

John watched them for another moment, registered that his attention had been caught and that he did not know why, and looked away.

He looked down at the dust motes. The dust motes were doing the thing they did. Tuesdays, he thought. The clustering on Tuesdays.

He opened his notebook. He wrote, in the small careful hand he had been using for his private dust-mote notes for six years:

Tuesday, May 27. Clustering confirmed, moderate, within normal range of elevated variance. Sub-pattern: secondary clustering around the new arrival at the window table, blue dreadlocks, yellow slicker. Possibly coincidence. Possibly the arrival displaced the air.

Also: I feel, faintly, a hum at the base of my skull that I have not felt since the text in December. The hum is not the same hum. Different vector. Still a hum.

Note: do not mention this to Elicia today. Tomorrow. Maybe tomorrow.

He closed the notebook.

The bell over the door chimed again.

This time it was Elicia.

She came in the way she came in everywhere these days — slower than she used to, more centered, wearing a denim jacket that had been repaired at the cuff and a scarf that was, John had noticed, the same scarf she had worn in the very first recording she had showed him last month, a scarf that had, she had told him, belonged to her mother. The scarf was, in the footage, the only thing Elicia wore that did not change costume.

He had asked her, when he saw it: is that the same scarf. She had said: yes. He had said: why. She had said: because whatever else I'm pretending not to be, I'm my mother's daughter. The scarf tells the camera not to let me forget.

He had not asked more. He did not need to.
Elicia crossed the café. Raised an eyebrow at the barista — Marco, who was fond of her in the careful way baristas were fond of regulars — and arrived at the small round metal table and sat.
I'm fourteen minutes late.
Twelve. I was generous.
You are always generous.
Not always.
Increasingly.

She looked at him. Her eyes, which had in Graustark always seemed to be framing the world for a shot, had lately, more often, been looking at the world instead. It was a small difference. It had changed everything about how her face was arranged around them.
What's wrong.
Why do you think something's wrong.
Because you're writing about dust motes again, and you only write about dust motes when you're avoiding telling me something.
John laughed the short surprised laugh again. The laugh had become, over the past seven months, something he did mostly around her.
You said, when you're ready, I should tell you first.
Yes.
I'm not ready. But I think I'm getting closer.
Elicia considered him for a long moment.
Is it about Graustark.
I don't think it's about Graustark anymore. I think it's about something that's starting somewhere else. I think Graustark was the pattern we all learned on. I think the pattern is — transferring.
Elicia looked at him. Then out the window, at the Montreal street with its trees beginning to go into their full green, at the ordinary Tuesday commerce of Saint-Viateur.
Transferring where.
I don't know yet.
John.
Yes.
When you find out, you tell me. That's the deal.
That's the deal.
She reached across the small metal table and took his hand. Briefly. Like a handshake at the end of a contract. Then she let go and picked up the menu she had already memorized.
I want a croissant. I've earned a croissant.
You have earned a croissant.
Get two.
One, Elicia.
One. Fine.

He got up to go to the counter.

As he passed the window table, the blue-dreadlocked woman looked up from her conversation for the first time.

She was, John registered with the small prickling attention of a journalist whose subconscious had just offered him a lead, very young — twenty-two, maybe twenty-three — and her eyes were the kind of eyes that had already seen more than twenty-two years normally allowed for. She met his gaze for a fraction of a second.

She did not, he was almost certain, know him.

But she registered him. The registering was the precise thing a hunter did when a hunter had been told to watch for a specific signature and had not yet seen it but was, nonetheless, on duty.

Then she looked back at the man across from her and the small moment — if it had been a moment — passed.

John ordered the croissants. He returned to the table.
What was that.
You saw it.
Yes.
I don't know what it was.
That woman is not from here.
No.
That woman is looking for someone.
Maybe.
That woman is in your pattern, John.

He looked at Elicia. He set down the small paper bag of croissants on the table between them. The shaft of late-morning sunlight was still falling across the table, the way it had been doing for six years of Tuesdays, the way it would do for the next hour at least before the angle of the light changed and the dust motes moved into their afternoon configurations.
Yes. I think she is.

Elicia broke the first croissant in half. Handed him the larger piece. Ate the smaller.

They sat together for a long quiet moment, chewing their bread, watching the light and the dust motes and the blue-dreadlocked woman at the window, who had gone back to her conversation, who was now laughing at something her tablemate had said — a real laugh, a young laugh, the kind of laugh that was evidence, to anyone paying close attention, that the older seriousness in her eyes was not, yet, the only thing she was.

John thought, she's not hunting. She's here for something else. The hunting is secondary.

He thought, and when she's done with whatever the primary thing is, she will be.

He thought, with the small clear honesty that had, in his Temperance period, become his working voice: I am glad, today, that I am sitting with Elicia. I am glad that the woman at the window has not, yet, come for me. I am glad that there are two warm croissants on the table. I am glad that I have six years of dust-mote notes and that they have, finally, begun to be about something.

He thought, this is what the Sun card is. Not the answer. Just the light on what is already here.

He broke a corner off his croissant. He ate it. The croissant was excellent.

Outside, the morning was in full bloom. Saint-Viateur did its Tuesday work. The bagel shop across the street began its lunch rush. A dog, tied to a bike rack, looked at every pedestrian with the bright unjudging attention dogs reserved for their favorite hour of the day.

The dust motes clustered. The pattern, in its own patient way, clarified.

Somewhere — south of here, south of this building, south of this country, somewhere on the long humid coast the older system was moving its pieces along — Joanna LeMoine's letter was arriving, this morning, at an address in New Orleans that had been waiting for it since 1987.

John did not, in the café on Saint-Viateur in late May, know this.

But the hum at the base of his skull — the small new hum, the one he had written about in his notebook ten minutes ago — registered the arrival.

The hum pulsed once. Then it steadied.
He drank the last of his espresso. He looked at Elicia. Elicia looked back.
Tell me.
Tomorrow.
Tomorrow, then.
Tomorrow.

He meant it. He would, in fact, tell her tomorrow. He did not yet know what he would be telling her, but he would, by the time the telling arrived, have figured it out.

Temperance had brought him to this table. The Sun, this morning, was lighting it.

Judgement was coming.

But not yet.

Not, at least, for the length of this one warm bright Tuesday in late May, in a café on Saint-Viateur, over two croissants, at the small round metal table in the back corner under the window where the dust motes did what they did.

Not yet.

Not yet.

The light held.
